text: The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum in the triangle between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to access the uterine artery. The uterine artery, identified crossing from lateral to medial, is clipped using vascular clips, with the ureter visible approximately one centimeter caudal and medial to the artery. Two clips are applied about 0.5 centimeters apart, and similar steps are repeated on the opposite side.

The surgeon identifies and clips both the uterine and vaginal arteries to minimize bleeding. An incision is made over the most prominent part of the fibroid along the posterior cervix, followed by enucleation of the fibroid from the myoma bed. The clipping effectively reduces bleeding during the dissection of the fibroid from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon lateralizes the right ureter at the level of the posterior cervix, moving it away from the surgical field to facilitate suturing during the myomectomy. The myoma is positioned more towards the right side, necessitating careful manipulation of the ureter up to the ureteric tunnel.

The surgeon uses a quill suture to begin suturing at the depth of the surgical field after mobilizing the ureter laterally to the level of the ureteric tunnel. The three-dimensional laparoscope provides enhanced visualization for precise suturing in the deep space.

The surgeon completes suturing in two layers to obliterate the myoma bed at the posterior cervix. Following the suturing, the remaining myoma is enucleated from the peritoneal cavity using a disposable morcellator blade.
